Organizer Young says the situa-
ton between the 3 local federal
unions and the contractors over en-.
forcement of the 2.75 wage scale is
too critical to talk about.


A. F. Gin, OF the principal EN-.
ployers of federal union labor, says
there is no trouble that he knows
of and that he is able to hire all
the men he needs.


Meanwhile, Young, with commit
tees from the unions and / from
the Central Labor union, is trying
to settle the friction that has re
suited in a strike of union and has
set 2 federal unions in opposition
to each other


The trouble has brought to the
surface lot of tangles that will
have to be Strengthened out, and IL
Is believed the final result will be
a reorganization under l head. II
is claimed ttat Federal union No,
11,624 Is composed Of foremen as
we'll as common laborers, in viola.
ton of the American Federation OF
Labor regulations, and that through
these foremen many members OF
the union are influenced into sup.
porting measures started In con
tractors interests. The accused
union disputes these allegations
and thus it goes.


When union No. 1922 tried to
enforce the s2.75 wage scale It is
claimed, the other union furnished
men at the $3.50 rate to the Indie.
pendent Asphalt Co., Of which Gill
is manager.


To reconcile these differences
and bring peace with the contrac
tors at the same time Is the task
set for Young. And he must also
adjust the new wage scale to con
tracts under the old scale that Il
will require all summer to com
plete.
